raw_case:
A large thyroglossal duct cyst with atypical features in a 73-year-old male: A case report


Introduction

Thyroglossal duct cysts (TGDCs) are congenital anomalies arising from incomplete obliteration of the thyroglossal duct (TGD), typically presenting in childhood. While rare in the elderly, they can occur in adulthood and may remain asymptomatic until complications such as infection or malignant transformation arise.

Presentation of case

A 73-year-old male presented with progressively enlarging left-sided neck swelling over 15 years. Clinical and radiological evaluation confirmed the diagnosis of a TGDC. Surgical excision was performed via the Sistrunk procedure, with post-operative histopathology confirming the pre-operative diagnosis of TGDC. The patient recovered well with no complications or recurrence.

Discussion

Clinical presentation of TGDC is typically an anterior neck enlargement or a non-tender midline neck mass, moving with swallowing, related to the hyoid bone. The typical differential diagnosis of TGDCs include cystic and midline neck masses. Atypically, our patient had a lateral cystic mass which was immobile with swallowing.

Conclusion

This case highlights the importance of considering TGDCs as a differential diagnosis in elderly patients despite their rarity. TGDCs may present with complications or atypical features of TGDCs which require more evaluation. Surgical intervention, specifically the Sistrunk procedure, remains the optimal management strategy for TGDCs, offering excellent outcomes and reducing the risk of recurrence.

Highlights

•TGDCs can occur in the elderly, expanding our understanding of this condition beyond its usual pediatric presentation.•Sistrunk procedure remains an effective and safe treatment for TGDCs, even in older patients.•Atypical presentation as a lateral mass and swallowing immobility is noted in patients with TGDC.1

Introduction

Thyroglossal duct cysts (TGDCs) are the most common congenital neck masses, arising from the persistent remnants of the thyroglossal duct (TGD) during embryonic development [1,2]. While typically presenting in childhood, TGDCs can also occur in adulthood, albeit less frequently, with cases in the elderly being exceptionally rare [1]. These cysts are characterized by a midline neck mass, often located near the hyoid bone, which moves with swallowing [1,3]. Most TGDCs are asymptomatic, but they can become infected, leading to abscess formation [3]. Malignant transformation of TGDCs is rare, occurring in less than 1 % of cases [3]. This report presents an unusual case of a large TGDC in an elderly patient.2

Presentation of case

A diabetic 73-year-old male with hypertension and a previous history of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) presented with a large neck swelling more pronounced at the left side of the neck, alongside with dysphagia, ear pain and feeling of discomfort in the pharynx in the last few months. Clinical examination showed a non-tender cystic swelling unmoving with swallowing, no other manifestations were observed (Fig. 1). This swelling developed gradually over the past 15 years. The patient reported inconsistent adherence to his prescribed medications. Blood tests of the patient were within normal limits. Computed tomography (CT) imaging revealed a large and well-defined thin-wall unilocular round cystic mass on the left side of the neck, which caused a deviation of the trachea and other components to the right, with no invasion of the surrounding tissues (Fig. 2). Clinical and radiological features matched the diagnosis of a TGDC. A FNA was performed but the histopathological report showed only benign cytology findings of inflammatory type (Fig. 3). A surgical excision was performed, removing the cyst, the middle third of the hyoid bone, and the tract (Sistrunk operation), The atypical presentation in our case, characterized by lateralization and immobility with swallowing, is likely attributable to the presence of extensive fibrosis surrounding the thyroglossal duct cyst. This fibrosis, a consequence of the cyst's prolonged presence, may have impeded the cyst's normal mobility during swallowing and contributed to its gradual displacement toward the lateral aspect of the neck. The total resected specimen was sent for histopathological evaluation. The Histopathological report of the sample confirmed the diagnosis of TGDC, showing a cyst wall lined by ciliated pseudostratified columnar cells, underlined with basal membrane and thin fibrous tissue with benign thyroid follicles with no malignancy (Fig. 4). The patient's wound exhibited complete healing without any complications. Subsequent follow-up assessments for a year indicated no evidence of recurrence.3

Discussion

Neck swellings typically arise from lymph nodes, thyroid, salivary glands and soft tissues [4,5]. TGDC is known to be the most prevalent among pediatric masses, mostly occurring as congenital anomalies but can also be observed at varying frequencies in adults, it can form anywhere on the migration route of the thyroid [3]. Our case discusses an unusual presentation of a large TGDC in an elderly patient who is 73 years old. This is unusual for this mass to appear in such ages especially when the patient reported that this swelling was gradually developed asymptomatically over the past 15 years [3]. While the classical presentation of a TGDC involves an anterior, midline neck swelling, mobile with swallowing and tethered to the hyoid bone [7], our case deviated from this typical pattern. The patient presented with a lateral cystic mass that remained immobile during swallowing, a finding likely attributable to extensive fibrosis surrounding the lesion. The differential diagnosis of lateral cervical masses is broad and encompasses a range of benign and malignant entities. These include, but are not limited to, hemangioma, branchial cyst, neoplastic changes in lymph nodes (metastasis and lymphoma), infective lymphadenitis, foreign body reaction, sialadenitis, neoplastic changes in salivary glands, and carotid aneurysm or carotid body tumor [6]. While clinical examination can often suggest a TGDC, definitive diagnosis and assessment of the mass extent necessitate imaging studies prior to surgical intervention [7]. This case serves to illustrate the variability in clinical presentation of TGDCs and underscores the importance of comprehensive diagnostic evaluation in cases of lateral neck masses. Ultrasound is commonly used to diagnose TGDC and CT imaging might be performed to evaluate TGDC and thyroid abnormalities [3]. TGDC appears on CT as a low-density thin-wall lesion routinely unilocular, on the migration route of the thyroid, often seen in midline related to the hyoid bone [7]. We have used CT to assess the diagnosis of TGDCs. Fine needle aspiration (FNA) is a quick and inexpensive, yet sensitive and accurate technique for neck masses evaluation [6]. we performed a FNA, but the cytology findings of the FNA was limited to inflammatory type cytology in our case. The Sistrunk surgical operation is the ideal management of TGDC which requires an extensive resection that includes a part of the tongue tissue and the middle third of the hyoid bone which reduces the recurrence rates whereas the recurrence rates reached (45 % to 55 %) in simple excision [3]. Surgical removal of the TGDC was performed in our patient to ensure the prevention of recurrent infections and the minimal risk of malignant development. There are few cases of TGDC in elderly people reported in literature including a case of an 85-year old male with a large long-standing TGDC, a case of a 65-year old male with a midline multilobular TGDC which had been growing over a year, and a case of 91-year old male with a large mass on the left side of the neck and mobile with swallowing [1,2,8].4

Conclusion

While the incidence of TGDC may be less frequent in geriatric patients, the Sistrunk procedure remains the gold standard surgical intervention for the majority of TGDC cases. Patients may present with serious complications including infection and malignant transformation. TGDCs may atypically present as a lateral mass and immobility with swallowing.

Abbreviations


TGDCsThyroglossal duct cystsTGDThyroglossal ductCOPDChronic obstructive pulmonary diseaseCTComputed tomographyFNAFine needle aspiration


Declaration of generative AI in scientific writing

None.

Methods

The Work has been reported in line with SCARE criteria [9].

Ethical approval

Not applicable because this paper has been reported from Hama University College of Human Medicine, Hama, Syria and our mentioned institution does not require ethical approval for reporting individual cases.

Funding

None.

Author contribution

Ahed Assaf, Mousa Barboura, Saja Karaja, Suleiman Khaddour, and Nour Maoud: Data Curation, Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft.Nazem Dadah: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.All authors read and approved the final manuscript.

Guarantor

Saja Karaja.

Research registration number

Not applicable.

Conflict of interest statement

None.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 73-year-old male
- **Chief Complaint:** Large neck swelling, more pronounced on the left side.
- **History of Present Illness:** Progressive left-sided neck swelling over 15 years, with additional symptoms of dysphagia, ear pain, and discomfort in the pharynx in recent months.
- **Past Medical History:** Diabetes mellitus, hypertension, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD).
- **Personal History:** Inconsistent adherence to prescribed medications.
- **Family History:** None reported.
- **Physical Examination:** Non-tender cystic swelling, immobile with swallowing, located on the left side of the neck.
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - Blood tests: Normal.
  - CT imaging: Large, well-defined, thin-walled unilocular cystic mass on the left side of the neck, causing deviation of the trachea without invasiveness.
  - Fine needle aspiration (FNA): Benign inflammatory cytology findings.
  - Histopathology: Cyst wall lined by ciliated pseudostratified columnar cells, supported by a basement membrane and benign thyroid follicles. No malignancy detected.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
- **Initial differential diagnoses** included common causes of lateral neck masses: thyroglossal duct cyst (TGDC), branchial cleft cyst, lymphadenopathy (infective or malignant), sialadenitis, and carotid body tumor. 
- TGDC was suspected despite the patient’s atypical lateral presentation and absence of mobility with swallowing. The longstanding, gradual progression and imaging features aligned with TGDC.
- Branchial cleft cyst was less likely due to lack of infection history or sinus formation.
- Lymphadenopathy (infective or malignant) was excluded due to the benign cytology and absence of lymph node characteristics on imaging.
- Sialadenitis was considered unlikely, as imaging did not reveal involvement of salivary glands.
- Carotid body tumor and aneurysm were excluded based on imaging findings that confirmed a thin-walled, unilocular cystic mass.
- The final diagnosis was solidified following surgical excision and histopathological evaluation, confirming TGDC.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Thyroglossal duct cyst (TGDC) with atypical lateral presentation due to extensive fibrosis and longstanding progression.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's tackle this case. The patient is a 73-year-old male with a 15-year history of progressive left neck swelling, more pronounced on the left. Recent symptoms include dysphagia, ear pain, and pharyngeal discomfort. His medical history includes diabetes, hypertension, and COPD, and he's inconsistent with meds. Family history isn't contributing here.

First, I need to think about the possible causes of a neck mass in an elderly patient. The fact that it's cystic and non-tender is a key point. Common cystic neck lesions include branchial cleft cysts, thyroglossal duct cysts, cystic hygromas (lymphangiomas), and possibly cystic degeneration of a thyroid nodule or a parathyroid cyst. However, the location and imaging findings might help narrow it down.

The CT shows a large, well-defined, thin-walled unilocular cyst on the left neck deviating the trachea. The FNA came back as benign inflammatory, and histopathology shows ciliated pseudostratified columnar epithelium with thyroid follicles. That's interesting. The presence of both respiratory-type epithelium (ciliated columnar cells) and thyroid tissue is a clue.

Thyroglossal duct cysts typically present midline, but can sometimes be lateral. However, they are lined by pseudostratified columnar epithelium, but usually don't have thyroid follicles in the cyst wall unless it's a thyroglossal duct cyst. Wait, but thyroglossal cysts are midline, near the hyoid. However, the lateral presentation here is confusing. Maybe a branchial cleft cyst? Branchial cleft cysts are usually in the lateral neck. The first branchial cleft cysts are near the ear, second are under the angle of the mandible, third/fourth are lower in the neck. But branchial cysts are lined by squamous or columnar epithelium, and don't typically have thyroid tissue.

Another thought: a lateral cervical cyst with thyroid follicles. That makes me think of a branchial cleft cyst with ectopic thyroid tissue, but that's rare. Alternatively, a cystic thyroid lesion, like a nodule, but the histopathology shows the cyst wall is lined by respiratory epithelium, not thyroid cells. Wait, the histo mentions the cyst wall has ciliated pseudostratified columnar cells (which is respiratory) and benign thyroid follicles. So the cyst is lined by respiratory epithelium and has thyroid tissue in the wall. That combination is characteristic of a branchial cleft cyst? Not exactly. Wait, branchial cleft cysts are typically lined by squamous or columnar epithelium, but not necessarily ciliated. The presence of thyroid follicles in the wall is unusual.

Wait, another possibility: a bronchial cleft cyst, which is a type of branchial anomaly. Fourth branchial cleft cysts are rare and occur in the lower neck. But the histology here includes thyroid follicles. Alternatively, a thyroglossal duct cyst that's off-midline? Thyroglossal cysts can sometimes be paramedian, but they're usually midline. But if the cyst has thyroid tissue in the wall, that's a hallmark of thyroglossal duct cyst. Thyroglossal cysts are remnants of the thyroglossal duct, which descends from the foramen cecum at the base of the tongue to the thyroid's final position. The duct normally disappears, but remnants can form cysts. These cysts are typically lined by respiratory or squamous epithelium and contain thyroid tissue in about half the cases. So if this cyst is in the neck, paramedian, with respiratory epithelium and thyroid follicles, then maybe it's a thyroglossal duct cyst, even though it's lateral. But thyroglossal cysts are usually midline. However, maybe in this case, the cyst is located off the midline. Alternatively, could it be a branchial cleft cyst with ectopic thyroid tissue? That's less common.

Alternatively, a branchial cleft cyst (like second branchial) would be in the lateral neck, along the anterior border of the sternocleidomastoid. The CT description here is a left-sided mass deviating the trachea, so maybe it's a larger cyst. The FNA was benign, and the histopathology shows respiratory epithelium and thyroid follicles. Another possibility is a laryngocele, but that's usually air-filled and related to the larynx. 

Wait, another entity: a branchial cleft cyst of the pyriform sinus, which is a type of fourth branchial cleft anomaly. These can present lower in the neck and may be associated with thyroid tissue. Fourth branchial cleft anomalies are rare and often present on the left side. The tract of a fourth branchial cleft cyst would course from the pyriform sinus apex, descend into the mediastinum, then loop around the aortic arch (on the left) or subclavian artery (on the right) before ascending to end in the neck. These can form cysts and might be associated with thyroid tissue if they are in proximity. However, the histology here shows thyroid follicles in the cyst wall, which might suggest a thyroglossal duct cyst. 

But the location is left lateral neck. Thyroglossal cysts are midline, but can be slightly off. Alternatively, a dermoid cyst or other congenital cysts. Wait, the presence of both respiratory epithelium and thyroid follicles is key. Thyroglossal duct cysts are lined by respiratory or squamous epithelium and have thyroid tissue in 40-60% of cases. So even if the cyst is paramedian, that's possible. Alternatively, a branchial cleft cyst with ectopic thyroid tissue, which is less common. 

The imaging shows it's a unilocular cyst, thin-walled, non-invasive. The histopathology confirms the lining and the presence of thyroid follicles. Putting it all together, the most likely diagnosis is a thyroglossal duct cyst. Even though it's on the left side, perhaps it's an atypical presentation. Alternatively, if the cyst is in the midline but causing deviation, but the problem is that the patient's swelling is more pronounced on the left. Hmm. Alternatively, maybe a branchial cleft cyst that has thyroid tissue. But the presence of thyroid follicles in the cyst wall is more indicative of thyroglossal duct cyst. 

Wait, another thought: ectopic thyroid tissue can form a cyst. But ectopic thyroid is usually in the midline as well. However, sometimes ectopic thyroid tissue can be found laterally, but that's less common. But in this case, the cyst is lined by respiratory epithelium, which is not typical for a simple ectopic thyroid cyst. 

So, considering all factors: the cyst is lined by respiratory epithelium and contains thyroid follicles. The combination of these two findings is classic for thyroglossal duct cyst, even if the location is lateral. However, thyroglossal cysts are midline. Maybe the cyst has extended to one side. Alternatively, perhaps this is a rare case of a lateral thyroglossal cyst. Alternatively, maybe the patient has a thyroglossal duct cyst that's presenting more laterally due to its size or position. 

Another possibility: a branchial cleft cyst with ectopic thyroid tissue. But the presence of thyroid follicles in the wall is not typical. However, if during embryogenesis, some thyroid tissue got incorporated into a branchial cleft cyst, that could happen. But this is not a classic finding for branchial cleft cysts. 

Given the histology is the key here, the presence of respiratory epithelium and thyroid follicles is highly suggestive of thyroglossal duct cyst. So even though the location is lateral, the diagnosis would be thyroglossal duct cyst. Alternatively, if it's a branchial cleft cyst, but the histology isn't matching as well. 

But wait, the CT shows it's on the left side, and the tracheal deviation. Thyroglossal cysts are usually midline, but in some cases, especially large ones, they can present asymmetrically. So perhaps this is a thyroglossal duct cyst that has grown large and is presenting more on the left side. The presence of thyroid follicles in the cyst wall would confirm that. 

So the final diagnosis is thyroglossal duct cyst. Even though the location is lateral, the histopathology is classic. Alternatively, a branchial cleft cyst with thyroid follicles, but that's less likely. The answer must be based on the histology findings. So the final answer is thyroglossal duct cyst.
results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient presents with a long-standing, progressive, left-sided neck mass. Key features include a cystic, non-tender, immobile swelling with dysphagia and tracheal deviation. Chronicity and imaging (thin-walled unilocular cyst) suggest a congenital or benign lesion.  
<step 2> Differential diagnoses for lateral cystic neck masses include branchial cleft cysts, lymphangiomas, thyroglossal duct cysts, and cystic thyroid lesions. However, thyroglossal duct cysts are typically midline, while branchial cysts lack thyroid tissue.  
<step 3> Histopathology reveals a cyst lined by respiratory epithelium (ciliated pseudostratified columnar cells) and benign thyroid follicles in the wall. This combination is pathognomonic for thyroglossal duct cysts, as thyroid tissue remnants are characteristic of embryologic thyroglossal duct migration.  
<step 4> Despite the lateral location, the presence of thyroid follicles within the cyst wall overrules atypical positioning, as large thyroglossal cysts can deviate or present asymmetrically. Benign cytology and imaging exclude malignancy or aggressive pathologies.  

### Answer:
Thyroglossal duct cyst

reasoning_eval.step: The patient presents with a long-standing, progressive, left-sided neck mass. Key features include a cystic, non-tender, immobile swelling with dysphagia and tracheal deviation. Chronicity and imaging (thin-walled unilocular cyst) suggest a congenital or benign lesion.

Differential diagnoses for lateral cystic neck masses include branchial cleft cysts, lymphangiomas, thyroglossal duct cysts, and cystic thyroid lesions. However, thyroglossal duct cysts are typically midline, while branchial cysts lack thyroid tissue.

Histopathology reveals a cyst lined by respiratory epithelium (ciliated pseudostratified columnar cells) and benign thyroid follicles in the wall. This combination is pathognomonic for thyroglossal duct cysts, as thyroid tissue remnants are characteristic of embryologic thyroglossal duct migration.

Despite the lateral location, the presence of thyroid follicles within the cyst wall overrules atypical positioning, as large thyroglossal cysts can deviate or present asymmetrically. Benign cytology and imaging exclude malignancy or aggressive pathologies.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Initial differential diagnoses included common causes of lateral neck masses: thyroglossal duct cyst (TGDC), branchial cleft cyst, lymphadenopathy (infective or malignant), sialadenitis, and carotid body tumor.
TGDC was suspected despite atypical lateral presentation and absence of mobility with swallowing; longstanding, gradual progression and imaging features aligned with TGDC.
Branchial cleft cyst was less likely due to lack of infection history or sinus formation.
Lymphadenopathy (infective or malignant) was excluded due to benign cytology and absence of lymph node characteristics on imaging.
Sialadenitis was considered unlikely as imaging did not reveal salivary gland involvement.
Carotid body tumor and aneurysm were excluded based on imaging findings confirming a thin-walled, unilocular cystic mass.
Final diagnosis was solidified following surgical excision and histopathological evaluation, confirming TGDC with atypical lateral presentation due to extensive fibrosis and longstanding progression.
